narrative: As a white male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 69.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the university's outcomes and reputation. This decision hinged primarily on your Year 3 school grades, which placed you in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and this was the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, while slightly under the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by nudging the model toward expecting success. Your Year 1 grades, also in a low decile compared to peers' averages around the 5th or 6th, added further concern and reinforced the risk of struggling on the bar. Other elements like your middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 27 (well below the average 36.7), and full-time student status—common among 93% of applicants—played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. In contrast to typical admitted students who boast stronger decile rankings and higher test scores, your profile signals higher risk, even as a full-time student like most. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag from patterns in thousands of similar cases. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, as even modest improvements in key areas have flipped outcomes for others in your position. You're clearly motivated to pursue law—use this feedback as a roadmap to build a stronger case next time.
